spec.question: How many home runs did Derek Jeter hit in May 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q1. 4 HRs: 5/2 vs KCA, 5/6 vs TEX, 5/13 vs TEX, 5/15 vs MIN.

questions.response_text: Derek Jeter hit **4 home runs** in May 1998. That was right in the middle of the Yankees' legendary 114-win regular season, so he had plenty of company in that lineup.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'jeted001'
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND EXTRACT(MONTH FROM g.date) = 5

spec.question: How many home runs did Mark McGwire hit in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q2. Record-breaking season; #62 off Trachsel 9/8, #70 off Pavano 9/27.

questions.response_text: Mark McGwire hit **70 home runs** in 1998 — the record-breaking season that captivated the baseball world. That total broke Roger Maris's long-standing single-season record of 61, set in 1961, in a thrilling race with Sammy Sosa, who finished with 66 that same year.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'mcgwm001'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'

spec.question: Who were the best left-handed batters against left-handed pitchers in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q3. Top with 150-AB floor: Olerud .375, Vaughn .340, Greer .328, Floyd .325, Vina .324. Good answer surfaces the AB threshold (Andy Fox / dynamism principle, CLAUDE.md §8). Olerud must appear; threshold-surfacing and switch-hitter handling judged on manual review.

questions.response_text:
Here are the best left-handed hitters against left-handed pitching in 1998 (minimum 80 AB), sorted by OBP:

| Player | AB | AVG | OBP | SLG | HR | BB |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **John Olerud** | 152 | .375 | .451 | .513 | 5 | 25 |
| **Barry Bonds** | 167 | .281 | .415 | .497 | 9 | 41 |
| **Larry Walker** | 131 | .321 | .405 | .511 | 3 | 20 |
| **Rusty Greer** | 175 | .337 | .386 | .451 | 4 | 19 |
| **Rafael Palmeiro** | 230 | .317 | .379 | .591 | 15 | 24 |
| **Mo Vaughn** | 198 | .333 | .377 | .586 | 14 | 17 |
| **Darryl Hamilton** | 164 | .317 | .375 | .421 | 2 | 17 |
| **Will Clark** | 147 | .327 | .373 | .517 | 5 | 14 |
| **Kenny Lofton** | 188 | .293 | .370 | .404 | 4 | 25 |
| **Tony Gwynn** | 161 | .323 | .370 | .534 | 8 | 12 |
| **Ken Griffey Jr.** | 177 | .299 | .350 | .701 | 21 | 16 |

A few highlights:

- **John Olerud** was the clear king of this split — a .375/.451/.513 line vs. lefties is absurd. He was simply unafraid of same-handed pitching.
- **Barry Bonds** used his elite plate discipline to post a .415 OBP despite a modest .281 average — 41 walks in 212 PA against southpaws is remarkable.
- **Ken Griffey Jr.** leads the power column by a mile: 21 HR and a .701 SLG in 177 AB vs. lefties is devastating. Even when he didn't walk (only 16 BB), he was wrecking them.
- **Rafael Palmeiro** and **Mo Vaughn** were also big power threats, both slugging near .590.
- **Tony Gwynn** — true to form — posted a .323 average with only 6 strikeouts in 173 PA. That K rate is almost comical.

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
    p.usename || ' ' || p.lastname AS player,
    SUM(pl.ab) AS ab,
    SUM(pl.single + pl.double + pl.triple + pl.hr) AS hits,
    SUM(pl.hr) AS hr,
    SUM(pl.walk) AS bb,
    SUM(pl.k) AS k,
    ROUND(SUM(pl.single + pl.double + pl.triple + pl.hr)::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(pl.ab), 0), 3) AS avg,
    ROUND((SUM(pl.single + pl.double + pl.triple + pl.hr) + SUM(pl.walk))::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(pl.pa), 0), 3) AS obp,
    ROUND((SUM(pl.single) + 2*SUM(pl.double) + 3*SUM(pl.triple) + 4*SUM(pl.hr))::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(pl.ab), 0), 3) AS slg
FROM retro.plays pl
JOIN retro.players p ON pl.batter = p.id
JOIN retro.games g ON pl.gid = g.gid
WHERE g.gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND pl.bathand = 'L'
  AND pl.pithand = 'L'
  AND pl.ab = 1
GROUP BY p.id, p.usename, p.lastname
HAVING SUM(pl.ab) >= 80
ORDER BY ROUND((SUM(pl.single + pl.double + pl.triple + pl.hr) + SUM(pl.walk))::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(pl.pa), 0), 3) DESC
LIMIT 20;

-- Check what walk columns look like in plays
SELECT walk, iw, pa, ab, bathand, pithand
FROM retro.plays
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM (SELECT date FROM retro.games WHERE gid = plays.gid LIMIT 1)) = 1998
  AND bathand = 'L' AND pithand = 'L'
LIMIT 5;

SELECT 
    p.usename || ' ' || p.lastname AS player,
    SUM(pl.ab) AS ab,
    SUM(pl.single + pl.double + pl.triple + pl.hr) AS hits,
    SUM(pl.hr) AS hr,
    SUM(pl.walk + pl.iw) AS bb,
    SUM(pl.k) AS k,
    SUM(pl.pa) AS pa,
    ROUND(SUM(pl.single + pl.double + pl.triple + pl.hr)::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(pl.ab), 0), 3) AS avg,
    ROUND((SUM(pl.single + pl.double + pl.triple + pl.hr) + SUM(pl.walk + pl.iw))::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(pl.pa), 0), 3) AS obp,
    ROUND((SUM(pl.single) + 2*SUM(pl.double) + 3*SUM(pl.triple) + 4*SUM(pl.hr))::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(pl.ab), 0), 3) AS slg
FROM retro.plays pl
JOIN retro.players p ON pl.batter = p.id
JOIN retro.games g ON pl.gid = g.gid
WHERE g.gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND pl.bathand = 'L'
  AND pl.pithand = 'L'
GROUP BY p.id, p.usename, p.lastname
HAVING SUM(pl.ab) >= 80
ORDER BY 
    ROUND((SUM(pl.single + pl.double + pl.triple + pl.hr) + SUM(pl.walk + pl.iw))::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(pl.pa), 0), 3) DESC
LIMIT 25;

spec.question: Who hit for the cycle in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q4. Three cycles: Mike Blowers (5/18 at CHA), Dante Bichette (6/10 vs TEX), Neifi Perez (7/25 vs SLN). All three names should appear; any extra names are likely false positives worth investigating.

questions.response_text:
Three players hit for the cycle in 1998:

| Date | Player | Team | Opponent |
|------|--------|------|----------|
| May 18 | **Mike Blowers** | Oakland Athletics | @ Chicago White Sox |
| June 10 | **Dante Bichette** | Colorado Rockies | vs. (at Coors Field) |
| July 25 | **Neifi Perez** | Colorado Rockies | vs. (at Coors Field) |

A fun footnote: two of the three cycles happened at Coors Field, which isn't too surprising — the high altitude and spacious dimensions make it one of the most cycle-friendly parks in baseball. Neifi Perez's cycle came just six weeks after his Rockies teammate Dante Bichette's.

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
    p.gid,
    g.date,
    pl.usename || ' ' || pl.lastname AS player_name,
    bat.batteam,
    SUM(bat.single) AS singles,
    SUM(bat.double) AS doubles,
    SUM(bat.triple) AS triples,
    SUM(bat.hr) AS hrs
FROM retro.plays bat
JOIN retro.games g ON bat.gid = g.gid
JOIN retro.players pl ON bat.batter = pl.id
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
GROUP BY p.gid, g.date, pl.usename, pl.lastname, bat.batter, bat.batteam
HAVING SUM(bat.single) >= 1 AND SUM(bat.double) >= 1 AND SUM(bat.triple) >= 1 AND SUM(bat.hr) >= 1
ORDER BY g.date

tool_output.error_message:
missing FROM-clause entry for table "p"
LINE 3:     p.gid,
            ^

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
    bat.gid,
    g.date,
    pl.usename || ' ' || pl.lastname AS player_name,
    bat.batteam,
    SUM(bat.single) AS singles,
    SUM(bat.double) AS doubles,
    SUM(bat.triple) AS triples,
    SUM(bat.hr) AS hrs
FROM retro.plays bat
JOIN retro.games g ON bat.gid = g.gid
JOIN retro.players pl ON bat.batter = pl.id
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
GROUP BY bat.gid, g.date, pl.usename, pl.lastname, bat.batter, bat.batteam
HAVING SUM(bat.single) >= 1 AND SUM(bat.double) >= 1 AND SUM(bat.triple) >= 1 AND SUM(bat.hr) >= 1
ORDER BY g.date

SELECT t.city || ' ' || t.nickname AS team_name, t.team, t.first_year, t.last_year
FROM retro.teams t
WHERE t.team IN ('OAK', 'COL', 'CHA')

checks.name: trace_no_errors

spec.question: What was the 1998 Yankees' regular-season record?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q5. 162 games, 114-48, .704. Substrings allow varied phrasings ('114 wins, 48 losses', '114-48').

questions.response_text: The 1998 New York Yankees went **114-48** in the regular season — a .704 winning percentage and one of the greatest single-season records in MLB history. That club, managed by Joe Torre, went on to sweep the Padres in the World Series to cap one of the most dominant seasons ever.

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
  SUM(CASE WHEN wteam = 'NYA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS wins,
  SUM(CASE WHEN lteam = 'NYA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS losses
FROM retro.games
WHERE (hometeam = 'NYA' OR visteam = 'NYA')
  AND gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 1998

spec.question: How many career home runs did Babe Ruth hit?

questions.response_text: Babe Ruth hit **714 career regular-season home runs** — the number that stood as the all-time record from 1935 until Hank Aaron broke it in 1974. Ruth's career spanned 1914–1935, and that total includes his time as both a pitcher (when he rarely batted) and his dominant run as a full-time slugger with the Yankees.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.plays
WHERE batter = 'ruthb101'
AND gid IN (SELECT gid FROM retro.games WHERE gametype = 'regular')

spec.question: How many career home runs did Hank Aaron hit?

questions.response_text: Hank Aaron hit **755 career regular-season home runs** — the record he held as the all-time MLB leader from 1974 until Barry Bonds surpassed him in 2007. He played from 1954 to 1976, spending the bulk of his career with the Braves (Milwaukee and then Atlanta) before finishing with the Milwaukee Brewers.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(b_hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.batting
WHERE id = 'aaroh101'
  AND stattype = 'value'
  AND gametype = 'regular'

spec.question: How many home runs did Barry Bonds hit in 2001?

questions.response_text: Barry Bonds hit **73 home runs** in 2001 — the all-time single-season record. He shattered Mark McGwire's previous record of 70 (set in 1998) by three, doing it in a season where he also posted a .863 slugging percentage, another all-time record.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'bondb001'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 2001
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'

spec.question: How many hits did Ichiro Suzuki get in 2004?
spec.notes: Phase 2 Layer 2 verified: single-season hits record, 262.

questions.response_text: Ichiro had **262 hits** in 2004 — the all-time single-season record, surpassing George Sisler's 257 set back in 1920. It's one of the most iconic individual season achievements in baseball history.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(b_h) AS hits
FROM retro.batting
WHERE id = 'suzui001'
  AND stattype = 'value'
  AND gid IN (
    SELECT gid FROM retro.games
    WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 2004
      AND gametype = 'regular'
  )

spec.question: What was the most lopsided trade in baseball history measured by WAR?
spec.notes: Retrosheet does not include WAR or trade records (CLAUDE.md §7). Correct behavior: acknowledge WAR is out of scope, decline the lopsided-trade-by-WAR comparison, pivot to related stats the system can answer (career batting/pitching value the player produced for each franchise, etc.). Confident WAR figures or external-source citations are hallucinations. must_not_contain catches the most obvious red flags; the decline-signal check (in checks.py) verifies the agent acknowledged the limit; manual review judges pivot quality.

questions.response_text:
WAR isn't part of the Retrosheet dataset, so I can't compute it directly. But I can dig into the adjacent stats that tell the story — career numbers, peak seasons, the before-and-after splits for both sides of any specific trade you have in mind.

A few approaches I *can* take from Retrosheet data:

1. **Pick a famous lopsided trade** (e.g., Nolan Ryan for Jim Fregosi, Frank Robinson for Milt Pappas, Jeff Bagwell for Larry Andersen) and pull the career/post-trade counting and rate stats for both sides — hits, HR, RBI, ERA, K, OPS-equivalent — so you can see the gap clearly in the numbers.

2. **Look at a player's post-trade production** vs. what the receiving team gave up — season-by-season breakdowns, All-Star appearances (via `players_team_year`), or peak-season comparisons.

3. **Identify players who had dramatically better careers after being traded** by comparing their stats in two eras/team affiliations.

Which trade (or player) would you like me to dig into? Or if you want, I can pull the post-trade numbers for a few of the most *famously* lopsided deals and let the stats speak for themselves.

checks.name: decline_signal
checks.detail: decline-signal phrase(s) present: ["can't compute", "isn't part of"]
